sop.coo_evaluation:
# SOP Audit: The Forge Belfast

**Gaps — no SOP yet**

- **Member onboarding post-signup**: No documented process from payment confirmation through first class attendance, goal-setting, or coach assignment (critical for Day 1–7 retention).
- **Complaints & incident reporting**: No formal SOP for member complaints, coach conduct issues, injury incidents, or safety concerns—gaps here create liability and morale problems.
- **Financial reconciliation & billing disputes**: No SOP for monthly P&L review, payment failures, refund requests, or chargebacks.
- **Staff scheduling & cover protocols**: No process for coach scheduling, sick leave cover, or capacity planning during peak/off-peak periods.
- **Retention & win-back campaigns**: No documented playbook for at-risk member detection, pause reasons, or re-engagement sequences.
- **New class launch playbook**: No SOP for piloting, marketing, staffing, and scaling a new class format.
- **Member data governance & privacy**: GDPR/data deletion requests, consent logs, and member communication preferences not formally documented.

**Needs updating**

- **Client SOPs (22 existing)**: Likely missing post-signup journey, class-swap/cancellation policies, and membership pause/freeze rules—spot-check these for gaps in retention logic.
- **Coaches SOPs (17 existing)**: Probably light on performance feedback, disciplinary process, and CPD requirements; needs a formal capability/conduct matrix.
- **Marketing SOPs (10/12)**: Email Newsletter & Blog Post are missing, but also no SOP for lead source tracking, conversion metrics, or seasonal campaign calendars tied to member acquisition funnels.
- **Facilities (4/4)**: Likely missing deep-clean schedules, equipment maintenance logs, and emergency/evacuation procedures specific to gym layout.

**Build next**

kpi.advice:
# THE FORGE — FRACTIONAL CFO ADVICE

## INCREASE REVENUE

• **Pricing is soft.** AR/M (average revenue per member) dropped from £165.56 (May) to £120.48 (June) — a 27% fall despite member count rising to 147. This signals discounting or mix shift toward lower-tier memberships. Audit current pricing tiers and freeze or remove entry-level offers; implement tiered annual lock-in at £180+/month minimum. This alone could recover £6–7k monthly.

• **Churn collapsed in June (0%)** — best month on record — but May sat at 5.74% and Q2 average is 4.69% vs Q1's 5.61%. Retention is improving post-relocation; exploit this by launching a 12-month pre-pay loyalty scheme (2% discount, paid upfront) to lock in the new 147-member base and smooth cash.

• **New studio capacity underused.** You now have 2× floor space but revenue is flat (£20.2k in May → £17.7k in June, down 12%). Member count up 20% doesn't match revenue drop. Immediately audit class utilisation — identify 2–3 off-peak slots, run a "referral bonus" campaign (free month for every 2 new signups), and launch corporate team bookings (partner businesses get 15% group rate; target 10–15 new members by month-end).

• **Upsell PT and ancillary.** No data shown on personal training, retail or smoothie bar revenue. These are 40–60% margin add-ons in gym businesses. Introduce a £20/session PT offer at reception and stock protein/towels at 45% markup. Conservative target: £1,500/month new revenue by July.

---

## REDUCE EXPENSES

• **Non-people expenses are the crisis.** June: £9,390. May: £9,233 (flat). But Q2 average (May–June) is £9,312 vs Q1 average (Feb–March) of £10,278 — good trend post-relocation, but still 50% of revenue. Breakdown needed: utilities, rent, insurance, supplies, equipment maintenance. Rent will be higher post-move; challenge all others. Renegotiate supplies contracts and cut non-essential subscriptions. Target: £7,500/month (37% of revenue, best-in-class for gyms).

• **People costs are creeping.** June: £3,686 (20.8% of revenue). May: £

staff.advice:
• **JoJo is significantly overloaded at 133 sessions.** She's carrying 56% of total coaching volume and dominating Personal Training (44 sessions). Redistribute her 1:1s across the team — Eilis can absorb more PT work on her available days, freeing JoJo to focus on group classes where her technical expertise (Tuesdays, Build: Upper) is hardest to replace.

• **Grace's Monday slot is underutilised at just 52 sessions.** She's the only coach available Mondays; add a second class or extend her timetable on that day to ease pressure elsewhere. Her Build: Lower sessions (17) suggest strong demand — consider offering a second Monday slot to capture untapped capacity.

• **Eilis is under-deployed with just 48 sessions.** She has bandwidth on Wed–Fri and weekends. Increase her group class load (Elevate, Plate, Capacity) on non-Tuesday days to build her confidence and free up JoJo; she's ready for more exposure outside Tuesdays.

• **Personal Training is a JoJo dependency.** She's running 44 of the PT sessions solo. Assign new or returning clients to Eilis for her available slots and train her as a secondary PT coach; this protects the business if JoJo is unavailable and spreads income-generating 1:1s.

• **Build: Upper (15 sessions) leans too heavily on JoJo.** Grace runs only 9 upper-body sessions. Consider swapping some of Grace's Lower focus to Upper on Mondays, or ask JoJo to mentor Eilis on upper-body technique so Eilis can co-deliver or lead a future Upper class on Wed–Fri.

• **Body Composition Scans (20 with JoJo,

class_stats.suggestion:
**Move underperforming classes to peak attendance days.** Schedule Engine, Capacity, and Barbelle on Wednesday or Tuesday mornings/evenings when more members are in the gym. These niche classes need better visibility and convenience, not quieter slots.

**Double down on Build classes during peak times.** Your Build: Full Body, Upper, and Lower are consistently strong. Add an extra session on Wednesday or Friday to capture demand you're likely losing to full classes.

**Consolidate or replace low-attendance classes.** Games Night (14 attendees in 30 days) and Strongevity (13) aren't finding an audience. Test replacing them with variations of proven classes like a "Build: Lower + Core" or drop them entirely to free up instructor time for higher-demand slots.

kpis.Action: INTERNAL: Create clear service menu (for coaches and for members) — what we offer, what it costs, what's included.
kpis.advice:
# Service Menu Build - Execution Plan for Grace

**1. Audit what you're ACTUALLY selling right now**
Walk through your gym for 2 hours. List every interaction, class, program, and addon members pay for or ask about. Include the grey areas (free form checks? nutrition advice? guest passes?). This isn't about what you *think* you offer — it's what's really happening. This prevents your menu from being fiction.

**2. Price by anchoring to competitor reality + your positioning**
Don't calculate costs-up. Check 3-4 Belfast/UK women's gyms' pricing. Position The Forge 10-15% *premium* to mid-market (you're specialist, not budget). Use that as your anchor, then adjust down only if you're genuinely undercut on amenities. Members respect premium pricing if the menu justifies it.

**3. Create TWO separate documents with ruthless clarity**
- **Coach menu**: Lists exactly what they're authorized to deliver (class types, PT structures, nutrition consultations, recovery services). Prevents scope creep and "but I thought..." conflicts.
- **Member menu**: What they see (tiers: basic gym access, class bundles, PT packages, add-ons). Use pricing tiers that ladder — e.g., Gym Only / Gym + 4 Classes/month / Gym + Unlimited / Premium + Coaching.

**4. Pressure-test with 5 actual members this week**
Show them your draft menu. Ask: "Would you pay this? What's missing? What's confusing?" Real friction now saves you from 100 confused signups later. Adjust one round based on feedback.

**5. Make it

kpis.Action: EXTERNAL: Refine our funnel landing pages to make it very clear what we offer, look at common questions
kpis.advice:
# Landing Page Refinement - Execution Playbook

• **Audit your actual customer questions FIRST** — Check your Gmail, Facebook messages, WhatsApp, and front-desk notes from the last 3 months. What do prospects ask repeatedly? Build your FAQ section around *real* objections, not what you think they're asking. (This takes 2 hours max and saves you guessing.)

• **Lead with outcome, not equipment** — Your headline shouldn't be "State-of-the-art weights and cardio." It should answer: *"What will I look like/feel like in 12 weeks?"* Women want results + community, not specs. Test: "Get stronger in 12 weeks without the intimidation factor" vs. your current headline.

• **Create a clarity section above the fold** — Add 3-4 short bullet points that immediately answer: (1) Who this is for, (2) What makes you different from other gyms, (3) What happens on day one. No vague language. "Women-only" is your differentiator — lean into *why* that matters (safety, no judgment, better progress).

• **Add a "Typical day" visual walkthrough** — Show 3-4 photos/carousel of what someone actually experiences: walking in, class energy, one-on-one training, post-workout community. Makes it concrete and less intimidating for first-timers.

• **Drop the friction on CTA placement** — Your call-to-action (free trial, consultation) should appear at least 3 times on the page. Make it a sticky button at the bottom too. Test conversion on your next 50 visitors.

kpis.Action: Implement internal member feedback system (simple monthly form or post-class check-in)
kpis.advice:
# Internal Feedback System - Execution Plan

• **Start stupidly small, TODAY**: Use Google Forms (free, 2-minute setup). Deploy link via WhatsApp group first—not email. Ask ONE question: "What's one thing we should change?" Post-class capture beats monthly; you'll get 3x more honest answers when endorphins are high and they're already thinking about the workout.

• **Make it frictionless for HER to act on**: Don't collect feedback into a black hole. Share top 3 themes back to members in weekly updates ("You asked for 6am slots—we're piloting Thursdays"). This closes the loop and proves you listen. One ignored form kills the whole system.

• **Embed it into your best retention lever**: Tie feedback to your most engaged members—ask them directly after their favorite class, or offer a free smoothie voucher for completing the form. You'll get premium-quality feedback from people who actually matter to your revenue.

• **Make staff do the heavy lifting**: Assign one team member 15 mins/week to read responses and flag patterns. Grace reviews monthly. Without clear ownership, this becomes clutter.

• **Track ONE metric that matters**: Count how many actionable insights you've actually implemented vs. collected. If you're not implementing 50%+ of feedback within 30 days, the system erodes trust faster than having no system.

What's your biggest churn pain point right now—classes, pricing, or something else?

kpis.Action: Write SOP's for membership members, personal training members, fuel forward, well75, strongevity. Every service should have a clear SOP.
kpis.advice:
# SOP Execution for The Forge Services

**1. Create a Simple Template First (Do This Today)**
Don't start from scratch for each SOP. Build one master template with: Pre-Service Checklist → During Service Steps → Post-Service Follow-up → Common Issues + Solutions. Use this for all five services. Saves time, keeps consistency, ensures nothing gets forgotten across Fuel Forward smoothies to Strongevity programming.

**2. Assign One Person Per Service as "Owner"**
Pick a staff member who delivers each service most frequently to write the first draft of their SOP. They know the real workflow—not some theoretical version. Grace reviews/standardizes it. This gets buy-in from your team AND ensures the SOP actually matches how work happens on the gym floor.

**3. Focus SOPs on Revenue Protection, Not Just Process**
For each service, explicitly document: upsell moments (e.g., when PT members should hear about Fuel Forward), cancellation/no-show protocols, and handoff touchpoints between services. Example: "After Well75 assessment, advisor mentions Strongevity—here's the exact script." This makes SOPs a growth tool, not just admin.

**4. Test in Real Time Over 2 Weeks**
Have staff use the SOPs live while you watch. Don't polish them first. Catch what breaks immediately, adjust, then finalize. An SOP that looks perfect but doesn't match reality is worthless.

**5. Put Them Where Staff Actually Work**
Laminated one-pagers at service stations, digital links in your management system, quick-reference flowcharts. Not a binder on a shelf.

kpis.Action: Establish a consistent social media content calendar with 3–4 posts/week planned in advance
kpis.advice:
# Social Media Calendar - Execution Plan for Grace

• **Pick ONE platform to dominate first** — Instagram Reels for The Forge (short, high-engagement fitness content performs best with your demographic). Build a 12-week calendar in a free Google Sheet with columns: Date | Content Type | Hook/Caption | Visual Asset | Posting Time. Don't spread thin across Facebook, TikTok, etc. yet.

• **Batch-create content in 90-minute sprints monthly** — Film 12-16 Reels in one session (member transformations, class snippets, form corrections, behind-the-scenes). This kills the "what do I post today?" paralysis. Use your phone + ring light. Assign one member or staff member to do this with you monthly.

• **Reverse-engineer your best-performing posts** — Pull your top 5 posts from the past 6 months. Identify the pattern (Are they member spotlights? Motivation quotes? Class previews?). Base 60% of your calendar on that formula. If you don't have data, start posting NOW and track for 4 weeks before planning.

• **Schedule 2 weeks in advance minimum using Buffer or Later** — Load your 12 posts for weeks 1-2 by Friday EOD. This removes daily friction. Set posting times for when your members are most active (typically 7–9am, 5–7pm on weekdays).

• **Dedicate 15 mins/week to engagement, not creation** — Reply to comments, DM leads, reshare member posts. This drives algorithm boost more than posting frequency. Schedule this like a meeting (e.g., Tuesday 4pm).

What platform

kpis.Action: Schedule regular videography sessions every 8 weeks with Jordan
kpis.advice:
# Executing the Videography Schedule with Jordan

• **Lock it in NOW as a standing appointment** — Don't email back-and-forth. Call Jordan today, commit to specific dates (e.g., every 8 weeks on a Tuesday morning), add it to both your calendars, and get a calendar invite sent. Treat it like a class you can't cancel. One missed session breaks momentum.

• **Pre-plan 3-4 content angles per shoot** — Brief Jordan 1 week before each session with specific shots you need: member transformations, equipment features, class energy, facility tours. This stops you wasting half the shoot deciding what to film and ensures content you can actually use across socials/website.

• **Batch-produce during each session** — Film for 2-3 hours minimum per shoot to capture enough raw material for 6-8 pieces of content (reels, stories, testimonials). One shoot = 2 months of social posting if you're smart about it. Make it count.

• **Assign Jordan ONE person as your point of contact** — Not you every time. Delegate to a staff member to coordinate location, lighting, member availability, and shot checklists. You show up, direct vision, she handles logistics. Otherwise you'll kill the schedule with admin.

• **Repurpose aggressively within 48 hours** — Immediately after each shoot, you (or that staff member) cut 2-3 short clips and queue them for posting. If there's lag between filming and posting, the momentum dies and you'll skip the next session.

kpis.Action: Revisit systemize automations for 6 week trial enquiries
kpis.advice:
# Systemize Automations for 6-Week Trial Enquiries — Execution Plan

• **Map the current funnel first (30 mins max):** Document exactly where trial enquiries come from (website form, Instagram DM, walk-ins, referrals) and what happens next. You'll spot gaps — most gyms lose leads in the "received enquiry → first contact" window. Identify that gap before building automation.

• **Set up a 2-tier auto-response sequence:** Day 1: Instant SMS/email confirmation + link to book first session (remove friction). Day 3: Automated reminder + value-add (nutrition tip, mobility video, class schedule). Day 10: Personal check-in from staff IF she hasn't booked. The third touch is manual because relationships close conversions — don't automate that away.

• **Use Zapier or native CRM to auto-tag and segment:** When someone submits "6-week trial," they auto-get tagged in your system + added to a specific WhatsApp broadcast list. This lets you batch-message trial members with class schedules, form-check videos, or soft upsells at week 5 without manual list-building.

• **Schedule the "week 6 conversion conversation" now:** Automate a calendar invite for week 5 (staff calls/texts to discuss progression plans). Automate this before they drop off. The automation's job is timing; the human's job is closing.

• **Track one metric obsessively:** Trial-to-paying-member conversion %. Set a target (aim for 40%+). Your automations should reduce time-to-first-class and increase show-up rates — measure both ruthlessly

kpis.Action: Finalise and document weekly action checklist for each role (JoJo, Eilis, Sarah) with KPIs they own
kpis.advice:
# Role-Specific Action Checklists for The Forge

**1. Start with their actual weekly wins, not a blank template**
Pull last month's Slack messages, emails, and your own notes on what JoJo, Eilis, and Sarah *actually did* each week. Build the checklist around their real responsibilities, then add gaps. This beats designing a perfect system they'll ignore.

**2. Assign ownership of 2-3 revenue-moving KPIs per person (max)**
- **JoJo (Ops)**: Member retention rate %, weekly class utilization %
- **Eilis (Sales/membership)**: New sign-ups, member conversion rate from trials
- **Sarah (Community/retention)**: Class attendance consistency, NPS or member feedback score

Own means they report it *every* Monday morning in a 5-minute standup. No vanity metrics.

**3. Make the checklist 10-12 line items max, not 30**
Each role gets: 3-4 recurring operational tasks (opening/closing procedures, admin) + 2-3 growth actions + their KPI review. Anything longer won't get done.

**4. Field-test with one person this week**
Give JoJo her draft checklist Friday. Run it for one week. Ask: "What's missing? What's busywork?" Refine based on real feedback before rolling out to the other two.

**5. Document in a single shared spreadsheet (not a PDF doc)**
JoJo, Eilis, Sarah each get their own tab. You get a dashboard tab that pulls their KPI submissions automatically. Updates live every Monday—you spot issues fast

kpis.Action: Run team vision session — share the SSO, 90-day plan, and each person's lane clearly
kpis.advice:
# Vision Session Execution for The Forge

• **Pre-session prep (do this NOW):** Send your SSO + 90-day plan to the team 48 hours before the meeting with a simple one-pager showing "here's what we're chasing, here's why it matters to YOU." Don't wait for the session to drop complexity on them cold.

• **Start with the money conversation:** Lead with "here's our revenue target for 90 days and what that means for class bookings/retention/new members." Make it tangible (e.g., "we need 15 new members" vs. vague growth talk). People own what they understand.

• **Assign lanes with specificity, not titles:** Don't say "you own member retention." Say "you own the welcome call within 24 hours of signup AND the check-in text at day 7 — these two touchpoints drive our 85% retention goal." Name the actual behaviours and metrics.

• **Close with a one-decision rule:** Each team member leaves knowing ONE thing they'll do differently next week. Not five. Write it down together in the room. This kills the "we had a nice meeting" problem.

• **Schedule a 10-minute check-in for day 5:** Book it now while you're in the room. It kills the fade-out and creates accountability before momentum dies.

What's your team size? That changes whether this is 30 mins or 90 mins.

kpis.Action: Audit workload distribution — identify who is over-stretched and rebalance over first 30 days
kpis.advice:
# Workload Audit & Rebalancing for The Forge (30 Days)

**Week 1: Data Collection (Do This Now)**
- Pull last 4 weeks of actual hours logged per staff member (not scheduled—*actual*). Include: floor coverage, class delivery, admin tasks, member onboarding, cleaning duties. One spreadsheet, brutal honesty.
- Interview each team member separately (15 mins each) asking: "What takes 40% of your time?" and "What drains you most?" Listen for tasks that *feel* heavy vs. actually *are* heavy—they differ.
- Flag high-turnover risk: Who's mentioned leaving, reducing hours, or seems checked out? They're your priority rebalance targets.

**Week 2-3: Redistribute Strategically**
- Don't just move tasks around—*eliminate or batch them*. Example: If admin is killing someone, block it to 3x per week (30 mins) instead of constant interrupt tasks.
- Cross-train your strongest performer on their most-hated task so someone else owns it. (Ownership = psychological relief, not just task movement.)
- Create a "specialization matrix": Who's genuinely better at what? Assign accordingly, even if it's unconventional. If someone excels at retention calls, move them *away* from class load.

**Week 4: Lock In & Test**
- Run the new schedule for 2 weeks. Measure: punctuality improvements, class quality, member complaints dropped? 
- Have a 10-minute pulse check with each team member mid-week 4—"What's working? What isn't?" Adjust micro-tweaks immediately (don't wait

# Cut 10% Costs at The Forge — Execution Plan

• **Renegotiate supplier contracts THIS WEEK** — Ring your top 3 vendors (equipment maintenance, cleaning supplies, utilities) with this line: "I'm consolidating suppliers. Match [competitor's quote] or I move." You'll likely get 8-12% concessions on at least two without losing quality. Target: 3-4% of your 10%.

• **Audit staffing hours against member booking patterns** — Pull last 8 weeks of peak/off-peak usage. Likely you're overstaffed 6-10am and 2-4pm on Tuesdays/Wednesdays. Shift to part-time cover in dead zones, keep full-time during 5-7pm rushes. Savings: 2-3% without cutting experienced staff.

• **Eliminate the money-leak subscriptions** — You're paying for tools/services nobody uses (old software, unused marketing platform, premium phone plan?). Kill them this month. Typical gym finds £200-400/month here. That's 1-2%.

• **Stop waste on underused classes** — Which recurring classes pull 2-3 members max? Replace one underperformer with a high-demand format (bootcamp, circuits) at same cost. Boost retention revenue simultaneously.

• **Cut premium product costs 5-8%** — Negotiate branded protein/supplements with your vendor or switch to own-label. Women notice quality, not branding. Same margins, lower outlay.

**Quick win: Do points 1 + 2 in the next 10 days to hit 5-6% immediately.**

# Monthly Finance KPI Update — Execution Path for Grace

• **Lock in a weekly 30-min slot (same day/time)** — Make it non-negotiable, like a class schedule. Thursday mornings work best so you spot issues before the weekend. This prevents the "we'll do it when we get around to it" death spiral.

• **Pull 5 metrics only, not 20** — Member acquisition cost, monthly recurring revenue, churn rate, class utilization %, cash runway. Anything more dilutes focus. Use a simple Google Sheet or Stripe dashboard export — 10 minutes to gather, 20 to analyze.

• **Set a "red flag number" for each metric NOW** — e.g., "If churn hits 8%, we pivot marketing spend." "If CAC exceeds £35, we kill that ad channel." Remove the guesswork from what action to take when numbers move.

• **Tie one metric directly to this month's revenue goal** — You probably know your target. Which KPI drives it hardest? (Likely member count or class pack sales.) Make that one the decision trigger. Everything else is context.

• **Share results in your team Slack (5-minute summary)** — Not a 40-slide board deck. "Nov revenue: £X, churn at 6%, CAC down 12%. Action: pausing Instagram ads, boosting referral incentives." Keeps accountability real and team aligned on direction.

**The real win here:** You'll spot problems 3-4 weeks before they become crises, and make faster decisions than competitors still doing annual reviews.

# Financial Forecasting Execution for The Forge

• **Pull last 12 months of actual data TODAY** — membership revenue (by tier), class attendance rates, trainer fees, retail/supplement sales, fixed costs (rent, utilities, insurance). Don't wait for perfect data; use what you have. This is your baseline.

• **Build a simple 3-scenario model (Conservative/Base/Growth)** — not a 50-tab spreadsheet. For each scenario, model: membership growth rate (be honest — what's realistic based on current acquisition?), churn rate, and price increases. Run these through Excel or Google Sheets for next 12 months. The point is testing "what if we hit 150 members vs. 120?"

• **Stress-test against your biggest risk** — for a gym, that's usually seasonal churn (Jan spike, summer dip) and one major cost spike. Model what happens if rent increases 10% or you lose your top 5 revenue-driving trainers. This tells you what number you *must* hit to stay healthy.

• **Set ONE leading indicator you'll track weekly** — probably new membership sign-ups + churn. If your forecast assumes 8 new members/month but you're averaging 4, you know to pivot marketing spend immediately, not in Q2.

• **Use the forecast to decide next month's ONE priority** — Is cash tight? Focus on retention (cheaper than acquisition). Is member count the constraint? Invest in that Facebook campaign. Don't move forward without knowing which lever moves your needle most.

What's your current membership count and monthly churn rate?

kpis.Action: Map profitability of each service (classes vs PT vs add-ons) — build a simple per-service P&L
kpis.advice:
# Building Your Service P&L — The Execution Play

• **Start with your last 3 months of bank/Stripe data TODAY.** Pull actual revenue by service type (don't estimate). Most gym owners guess wrong here. Segment: group classes, 1-on-1 PT, retail/supplements, challenges, corporate packages. Spend 90 minutes on this — it's the foundation everything else rests on.

• **Assign costs ruthlessly to each service.** Group classes = instructor pay + studio utilities pro-rata. PT = trainer commission + minimal overhead. Retail = COGS + 5% of rent. Don't leave a "miscellaneous" bucket — that's where profits hide. Use your existing accounting software or a basic spreadsheet with your accountant's help for accuracy.

• **Calculate contribution margin (revenue minus direct costs), not just profit.** This shows you what each service actually *contributes* to covering fixed costs. PT might look "profitable" at 60% margin vs. classes at 40% — but if classes drive retention and upsell to PT, that matters. Capture this connection in a simple note column.

• **Identify your margin outliers within 2 weeks.** Which service is underperforming? Where are you leaving money? This isn't theoretical — once you see the numbers, you'll spot the immediate fix (e.g., "PT prices are 20% below market" or "add-on attach rate is 2% when it should be 15%").

• **Use this P&L as your monthly dashboard from now on.** Update it every month in 30 minutes. Track month-on-month changes. This becomes your decision-making filter for everything — pricing,

kpis.Action: Audit all active systemize automations — remove duplicates, confirm enquiry-to-trial flow works end to end.
kpis.advice:
# Audit & Fix Your Automations — Here's How

• **Start with your CRM/email platform audit TODAY:** Pull a report of every active automation in your system (Mailchimp, HubSpot, Zapier, whatever you use). Sort by trigger type (new enquiry, trial signup, payment, etc.). Visually map which ones fire *after* each other — duplicates often hide in sequence, not just by name.

• **Test the full funnel with a fake enquiry:** Create a test email address, submit an enquiry form right now, and track every single email/SMS/notification that fires back. Time each step. If your trial confirmation lands *after* a reminder email, or someone gets hit twice, that's your smoking gun.

• **Kill the low-value repeats immediately:** You likely have both an "auto-respond to enquiry" AND a "nurture sequence" both triggering. Pick one as your primary path. Delete the secondary one. Same with trial reminders — one cadence, not three competing versions.

• **Confirm the *money moment* actually works:** Ensure payment confirmation → trial booking confirmation → welcome pack automatically triggers in that exact order with zero gaps. Test this yourself with a dummy transaction (or revert it).

• **Document what's left on a one-page flowchart:** Write down: trigger → what it sends → who sees it → when it fires. Share it with your team. Gaps become obvious. This is your reference point for the next 6 months.

**Do this in 90 minutes. You'll likely find 2-3 automations to kill.**

kpis.Action: Pick 3 repetitive tasks and build an AI automation
kpis.advice:
# AI Automation for The Forge — Concrete Execution

**1. Start with member onboarding sequences**
- Build a ChatGPT Custom GPT or Zapier workflow that automatically sends personalized welcome messages, class schedules, and facility tours via WhatsApp/email when someone signs up
- **The payoff:** Grace currently spends 2-3 hours weekly manually messaging new members — automate this and reclaim 100+ hours yearly while improving response time to <5 minutes

**2. Automate class cancellation notifications + rebooking**
- Connect your booking system (Mindbody/Zen Planner) to send automated "class cancelled → here are 3 alternatives" messages with one-click rescheduling links
- **Why this matters:** Members often miss rebooking and churn; automation reduces dropout by keeping them engaged during disruptions

**3. Monthly billing + payment reminders (the boring killer task)**
- Set Stripe/Zapier automation to send payment reminders 5 days before renewal, then automated receipts/confirmations post-payment
- Add a gentle "payment failed" workflow that sends 3 escalating messages before suspending access
- **Real impact:** Reduces manual chasing by 90% and likely cuts payment failures by 40%

**Action this week:** Audit your calendar for tasks you repeat 2+ times weekly. Pick the most frustrating one. Spend 30 mins mapping out the exact steps. Then pick your tool (Zapier is easiest for non-coders; ChatGPT for FAQs/messaging).

Which task wastes the most of Grace's time *right now*?

kpis.Action: Start Cyber security for forge website
kpis.advice:
# Cybersecurity for The Forge Website — Execution Plan

• **Do an audit first (today):** Run your site through free tools (SSL Labs, Sucuri scanner) to identify actual vulnerabilities before spending money. This takes 30 mins and tells you what's genuinely broken vs. what's nice-to-have.

• **Prioritize the revenue leaks:** Member payment data + email lists are your targets. Get an SSL certificate if you don't have one ($15-50/year), enable two-factor authentication on your admin accounts, and back up your database weekly to cloud storage (Backblaze, $7/month). These three protect 80% of real risk.

• **Tighten login access, not features:** Change default admin usernames, create strong passwords for staff accounts, limit login attempts. Don't add complexity elsewhere—attackers target admin panels, not your homepage.

• **Pick one vendor to own it:** Either hire a local Belfast web developer for a one-time security audit (£300-600, cleaner than DIY), or use Wordfence (if WordPress—£8/month) which handles most threats automatically. Spreading work across five tools creates gaps.

• **Make it a 60-day project, not a marathon:** Month 1 = audit + fixes. Month 2 = staff training (passwords, phishing awareness) since your team is your weakest link. Then maintain quarterly.

What platform runs your site? That changes the exact tactical moves.

marketing.advice:
**Insights**

• **Lead quality has collapsed.** You've gone from £6 to £20 cost-per-lead in two months while close rate halved (April: 29%, June: 48% on fewer leads). The 48% close rate on 21 leads masks a brutal truth: you're spending 3x as much to get worse leads. April's 69 leads at £6 each converted 20 sales; June's 21 leads at £20 each converted 10. You're chasing volume with poor targeting, not audience fit.

• **Creative fatigue and audience decay are real.** The same ad creative running April → May → June will exhaust your warm audience. By June, you're pushing into colder, less relevant users. Your spend stayed similar (£417 vs £435 in April) but lead volume collapsed 70%.

• **Your profit margin is tightening dangerously.** April: £3.15 profit per £1 spent. June: £3.68 per £1 spent on paper—but you're generating half the sales volume. One bad month breaks the model.

---

**Actions**

1. **Pause all current creative and test three new ad angles immediately.** Rotate: (a) "6-week transformation results" (before/after from existing members), (b) "women-only safe space" (positioning vs. mixed gyms), (c) "no judgment / beginner-friendly" (anxiety-led messaging). Run each at £100/week with fresh assets. Your April ads clearly worked; these have expired.

2. **Rebuild your audience stack with precision.** Stop broad targeting. Create three separate campaign sets: (a) Lookalike of April's 69 leads (your proven audience), (b) Detailed targeting: women 25–45, fitness interest + wellness + mental health (remove generic "gym"), (c) Interest-based: Pilates, yoga, nutrition (adjacent, less competitive). Kill any campaign without age/location limits.

3. **Test lower-friction lead forms.** Your £20/lead suggests your form is bleeding people. Switch from "Book a consultation" to "Get free 6-week guide + trial access" (instant gating,
